message.content:
You are an independent blind verifier (verifier A) for the hc-assay hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-10). Decide TRUE_POSITIVE (genuine production-readiness defect to fix) or FALSE_POSITIVE (false/already-mitigated/out-of-scope-by-design/not real). Out-of-scope: dataset-agnostic opaque inputs; optional import-guarded backends; intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts (but a NEW leaked PII/secret class IS in scope); on-box data-sovereignty; documented honest-scope. Read real code and REPRODUCE where testable (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python ...; "python -m pytest"; gh). Judge ONLY REL-1.

FINDING REL-1 (low): SIGTERM handler swallows the signal when a prior C-installed handler makes getsignal() return None
Location: src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py _install_flush_on_exit._handler
Claim: The pass-8 K-REL-1 fix handles prior==SIG_DFL (re-deliver) and callable prior (chain). But signal.getsignal() returns None when the previous handler was installed from NON-Python (C) code. None is neither callable nor == SIG_DFL, so the handler flushes and RETURNS without terminating or chaining — swallowing SIGTERM again in that case. VERIFY: is None a reachable getsignal() return, and does the handler's elif (prior==SIG_DFL) miss it, falling through to no termination? Assess realism (a C-installed SIGTERM handler before bootstrap_tracing).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Self-hosted tracing — OpenTelemetry → a local collector (ADR-0003).
2	
3	Ported and generalized from the prior platform's Phoenix/OTel bootstrap. Everything is
4	on-box: spans export over OTLP to a loopback-bound collector; nothing leaves the machine.
5	OpenTelemetry and the Phoenix collector are optional (the ``observability`` extra) and
6	imported lazily, so this module loads — and the engine runs untraced — with neither present.
7	
8	The reasoning seam already emits spans through the global OTel tracer provider; calling
9	:func:`bootstrap_tracing` once at startup wires that global provider at a local collector so
10	those spans land somewhere. Without the extra (or with ``ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING`` set),
11	tracing degrades to a silent no-op.
12	"""
13	
14	from __future__ import annotations
15	
16	import atexit
17	import logging
18	import os
19	import signal
20	import threading
21	from contextlib import contextmanager
22	from typing import Any, Iterator, Mapping, Protocol
23	
24	from assay_engine._local import require_loopback_host
25	
26	_log = logging.getLogger("assay_engine.observability")
27	
28	# Local collector endpoint (loopback-enforced).
29	TRACING_HOST = os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_HOST", "localhost")
30	
31	
32	def _int_env(name: str, default: str) -> int:
33	    """Parse an int env var with a clear error naming the var (#CV-S-1, same class as #H-022).
34	
35	    A bare ``int(os.environ[...])`` at import time raises an opaque "invalid literal for int()"
36	    that names neither the var nor fires inside any handler.
37	    """
38	    raw = os.environ.get(name, default)
39	    try:
40	        return int(raw)
41	    except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
42	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}") from exc
43	
44	
45	TRACING_PORT = _int_env("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "6006")
46	PROJECT_NAME = os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_PROJECT", "assay")
47	
48	_provider: Any = None
49	# Guards the _provider check-then-set so concurrent bootstrap_tracing() calls don't race.
50	_provider_lock = threading.Lock()
51	
52	
53	def _disabled() -> bool:
54	    return os.environ.get("ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING", "").strip().lower() in {"1", "true", "yes", "on"}
55	
56	
57	def tracing_base_url() -> str:
58	    require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
59	    return f"http://{TRACING_HOST}:{TRACING_PORT}"
60	
61	
62	def tracing_endpoint() -> str:
63	    """OTLP/HTTP traces endpoint at the local collector (loopback-enforced)."""
64	    return f"{tracing_base_url()}/v1/traces"
65	
66	
67	def bootstrap_tracing() -> Any:
68	    """Idempotently wire the global OTel tracer provider to the local collector.
69	
70	    Returns the provider, or ``None`` if tracing is disabled, the extra is not installed, or
71	    setup fails — tracing must never be load-bearing, so this degrades gracefully.
72	    """
73	    global _provider
74	    if _disabled():
75	        return None
76	    # Lock the check-then-set so two threads can't both register the global provider.
77	    with _provider_lock:
78	        if _provider is not None:
79	            return _provider
80	        try:
81	            require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
82	            # Bound the OTLP export so a downed collector can never hang teardown (#O3).
83	            os.environ.setdefault("OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_TIMEOUT", "2")
84	            from phoenix.otel import register
85	
86	            provider = register(
87	                project_name=PROJECT_NAME,
88	                endpoint=tracing_endpoint(),
89	                protocol="http/protobuf",  # pin: endpoint is .../v1/traces — never flip to gRPC
90	                set_global_tracer_provider=True,
91	                batch=True,  # async BatchSpanProcessor — never block the run on export (#O2)
92	                verbose=False,  # no stdout banner from a library context
93	            )
94	            _provider = provider
95	            _warn_if_not_global(provider)
96	            _instrument_langchain()
97	            _install_flush_on_exit(provider)
98	            return _provider
99	        except Exception as exc:
100	            # Missing extra / collector down / registration race — run untraced. Graceful
101	            # degradation, but no longer SILENT (pass 3, #F-044): a misconfigured
102	            # ASSAY_TRACING_HOST/PORT or a missing observability extra now leaves a diagnosable
103	            # warning instead of an inexplicably untraced run.
104	            _log.warning(
105	                "assay tracing: setup failed, running untraced (%s: %s)",
106	                type(exc).__name__,
107	                exc,
108	            )
109	            return None
110	
111	
112	def _warn_if_not_global(provider: Any) -> None:
113	    """If another provider already won the global slot, our manual spans would vanish (#O6)."""
114	    try:
115	        from opentelemetry import trace
116	
117	        if trace.get_tracer_provider() is not provider:
118	            _log.warning(
119	                "assay tracing: another OpenTelemetry provider holds the global slot; "
120	                "manually-emitted spans may not reach the local collector"
121	            )
122	    except Exception:
123	        pass
124	
125	
126	def _instrument_langchain() -> None:
127	    """Capture LangChain auto-spans (the bulk reasoning tier uses LangChain) — best-effort (#O5)."""
128	    try:
129	        from openinference.instrumentation.langchain import LangChainInstrumentor
130	
131	        LangChainInstrumentor().instrument(skip_dep_check=True)
132	    except Exception:
133	        pass
134	
135	
136	def _install_flush_on_exit(provider: Any) -> None:
137	    """Flush buffered spans on clean exit (atexit) and on SIGTERM (#O3).
138	
139	    atexit does not run on ``kill``/container-stop, so a main-thread SIGTERM handler also
140	    flushes+shuts down. Both are guarded — tracing teardown must never raise.
141	    """
142	    flush = getattr(provider, "force_flush", None)
143	    if not callable(flush):
144	        return
145	    atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))
146	    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
147	        try:
148	            prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)
149	
150	            def _handler(signum: int, frame: Any) -> None:
151	                _safe(flush)
152	                _safe(getattr(provider, "shutdown", lambda: None))
153	                # Preserve the ORIGINAL disposition (#K-REL-1). If a Python handler pre-existed,
154	                # chain to it. Otherwise prior is SIG_DFL/SIG_IGN — both NON-callable — and simply
155	                # returning here would SWALLOW the signal: we replaced SIGTERM's default
156	                # terminate-the-process action with a flush-and-return, so `docker stop` / `kill` /
157	                # k8s would hang until SIGKILL escalation. Re-establish and re-deliver the default
158	                # action so the process still dies on SIGTERM (SIG_IGN stays ignored).
159	                if callable(prior):
160	                    prior(signum, frame)
161	                elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL:
162	                    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal.SIG_DFL)
163	                    os.kill(os.getpid(), signum)  # re-deliver → default terminate action runs
164	
165	            signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)
166	        except (ValueError, OSError):  # pragma: no cover - not on main thread / unsupported
167	            pass
168	
169	
170	def _safe(fn: Any) -> None:
171	    try:
172	        fn()
173	    except Exception:
174	        pass
175	
176	
177	@contextmanager
178	def run_trace_context(run_id: str | None) -> Iterator[None]:
179	    """Stamp ``run_id`` onto every span emitted inside the block (cross-store correlation).
180	
181	    A no-op when ``run_id`` is None or OpenTelemetry is absent.
182	    """
183	    if run_id is None:
184	        yield
185	        return
186	    try:
187	        from opentelemetry import baggage, context
188	    except ImportError:
189	        yield
190	        return
191	    token = context.attach(baggage.set_baggage("assay.run_id", run_id))
192	    try:
193	        yield
194	    finally:
195	        context.detach(token)
196	
197	
198	# OpenInference requires every span carry a span kind so Phoenix classifies it (CHAIN/LLM/
199	# AGENT/TOOL); a plain OTel span with no kind renders as UNKNOWN in the Phoenix UI. We set the
200	# raw semantic-convention attribute directly (no openinference import needed for a manual span).
201	_OPENINFERENCE_SPAN_KIND = "openinference.span.kind"
202	
203	
204	# Structural Protocol only — adapter/seam validation is behavior-based, not isinstance (#148).
205	class Tracer(Protocol):
206	    @contextmanager
207	    def span(
208	        self, name: str, attributes: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, *, kind: str = "CHAIN"
209	    ) -> Iterator[None]: ...
210	
211	
212	class OtelTracer:
213	    """A tracer that emits OpenTelemetry spans via the global provider (no-op if OTel absent).
214	
215	    Use this where a component wants explicit spans rather than relying on auto-instrumentation;
216	    it honors whatever provider :func:`bootstrap_tracing` registered. Each span is stamped with
217	    its OpenInference ``kind`` so Phoenix classifies it (default ``CHAIN``).
218	    """
219	
220	    @contextmanager
221	    def span(
222	        self, name: str, attributes: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, *, kind: str = "CHAIN"
223	    ) -> Iterator[None]:
224	        try:
225	            from opentelemetry import trace
226	        except ImportError:
227	            yield
228	            return
229	        tracer = trace.get_tracer("assay_engine")
230	        # start_as_current_span defaults record_exception=True and set_status_on_exception=True,
231	        # so an exception escaping the body is automatically recorded as a span event and the span
232	        # status is set to ERROR with the exception text (verified in test_observability) — #F-005
233	        # was a false positive; no manual error-recording is needed here.
234	        with tracer.start_as_current_span(name) as sp:
235	            sp.set_attribute(_OPENINFERENCE_SPAN_KIND, kind)
236	            for k, v in (attributes or {}).items():
237	                sp.set_attribute(k, v)
238	            yield
239	
240	
241	class UnconfiguredTracer:
242	    """Fail-loud placeholder for contexts that must not silently drop spans."""
243	
244	    @contextmanager
245	    def span(
246	        self, name: str, attributes: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None, *, kind: str = "CHAIN"
247	    ) -> Iterator[None]:
248	        raise NotImplementedError(
249	            "no tracer configured — call bootstrap_tracing() and use OtelTracer, or supply a "
250	            "test double"
251	        )
252	        yield  # pragma: no cover - keeps this a generator/contextmanager
253

content.text:
The finding is about lines 159-163. Let me analyze the handler logic and verify whether `getsignal()` can return `None`.

The handler logic:
```python
if callable(prior):
    prior(signum, frame)
elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL:
    signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal.SIG_DFL)
    os.kill(os.getpid(), signum)
```

The finding claims `signal.getsignal()` can return `None` when the prior handler was installed from C code (non-Python). Let me verify this is documented behavior.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
import signal
help(signal.getsignal)
"
input.description: Check getsignal docstring for None return

content.content:
Help on function getsignal in module signal:

getsignal(signalnum)
    Return the current action for the given signal.

    The return value can be:
      SIG_IGN -- if the signal is being ignored
      SIG_DFL -- if the default action for the signal is in effect
      None    -- if an unknown handler is in effect
      anything else -- the callable Python object used as a handler

content.text: Confirmed: `getsignal()` returns `None` "if an unknown handler is in effect" — i.e., a handler installed from C code that isn't a known Python signal handler. Let me reproduce the swallowing behavior.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
import signal, os

# Simulate: getsignal returns None (C-installed handler)
prior = None

# Replicate the handler's branch logic
def simulate(prior, signum=signal.SIGTERM):
    if callable(prior):
        return 'CHAIN to prior'
    elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL:
        return 'RE-DELIVER default (terminates)'
    else:
        return 'NEITHER branch taken -> handler returns -> SIGNAL SWALLOWED'

print('prior=None      ->', simulate(None))
print('prior=SIG_DFL   ->', simulate(signal.SIG_DFL))
print('prior=SIG_IGN   ->', simulate(signal.SIG_IGN))
print('SIG_IGN==SIG_DFL?', signal.SIG_IGN == signal.SIG_DFL)
print('None==SIG_DFL?   ', None == signal.SIG_DFL)
print('callable(None)?  ', callable(None))
"

content.content:
prior=None      -> NEITHER branch taken -> handler returns -> SIGNAL SWALLOWED
prior=SIG_DFL   -> RE-DELIVER default (terminates)
prior=SIG_IGN   -> NEITHER branch taken -> handler returns -> SIGNAL SWALLOWED
SIG_IGN==SIG_DFL? False
None==SIG_DFL?    False
callable(None)?   False

content.text:
The logic confirms the bug. Now the key question: is `prior == None` realistic? It requires a C-installed (non-Python) SIGTERM handler before `bootstrap_tracing`. Let me also note `SIG_IGN` falls through too — but that's intentional per the comment ("SIG_IGN stays ignored"), which is correct behavior. The `None` case is the genuine gap.

Let me assess realism. Can I actually trigger `getsignal()` returning `None`?

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
import signal
# In practice, can pure-Python observe None? Hard without a C extension installing one.
# But document the realistic vector: native extensions / runtimes that install C handlers.
print('default SIGTERM:', signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM))
print('default SIGTERM == SIG_DFL:', signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM) == signal.SIG_DFL)
"

content.content:
default SIGTERM: 0
default SIGTERM == SIG_DFL: True

content.text:
Now let me assess realism of a C-installed SIGTERM handler. Examples that install C-level SIGTERM handlers before Python user code: gunicorn/uvicorn worker bootstraps in some configs, certain native runtimes, embedded Python, gRPC/absl, faulthandler-adjacent libs, or any C extension calling `sigaction` directly. These produce `getsignal() == None`.

Let me check whether the project itself plausibly runs under such a context (the module docstring mentions Phoenix/OTel, gRPC).

# absl (common transitive dep via grpc/tensorflow) installs C signal handlers

    print('absl present')
except Exception as e:
    print('no absl:', type(e).__name__)

content.text:
The finding is verified. Let me consolidate my assessment.

**Code analysis confirms the bug exists:**

1. `signal.getsignal()` officially returns `None` "if an unknown handler is in effect" (verified from the docstring) — this is precisely the case when a SIGTERM handler was installed from non-Python (C) code before `bootstrap_tracing()`.

2. Reproduced the branch logic: when `prior is None`, neither branch fires:
   - `callable(None)` → `False` (skips chain)
   - `None == signal.SIG_DFL` → `False` (skips re-deliver)
   - The `_handler` flushes and returns, leaving the process alive — SIGTERM is swallowed, the exact regression K-REL-1 was meant to fix.

3. The handler does NOT restore the prior C handler either, so even re-raising wouldn't reach it; the original C-installed disposition is permanently lost the moment `signal.signal(SIGTERM, _handler)` runs at line 165.

**Realism assessment:** A C-installed SIGTERM handler before bootstrap is uncommon but real. Native runtimes/extensions (gRPC, absl, TensorFlow, some embedded-Python or supervisor shims) install handlers via `sigaction` directly, yielding `getsignal() == None`. The module's own docstring targets exactly the `docker stop`/k8s teardown path where a swallowed SIGTERM causes a hang until SIGKILL escalation — a genuine production-readiness defect with operational impact (delayed shutdown, lost final span flush guarantees, masked termination).

Note: the `SIG_IGN` fall-through is intentional and correct (comment: "SIG_IGN stays ignored"), so that is not a defect. The `None` case is the unhandled gap. The proper fix is to treat `prior is None` like `SIG_DFL` (reset to default and re-deliver) so the process still terminates.

input.reasoning: Verified in src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py (_install_flush_on_exit._handler, lines 159-163). signal.getsignal() officially returns None "if an unknown handler is in effect" — confirmed from the live docstring — which occurs when SIGTERM was installed by non-Python (C) code before bootstrap_tracing(). Reproduced the handler branch logic: for prior=None, callable(None) is False (skips the chain branch) and None == signal.SIG_DFL is False (skips the re-deliver branch), so _handler flushes and RETURNS without terminating or chaining. Because line 165 already replaced SIGTERM's terminate action with this flush-and-return handler, the signal is swallowed: docker stop / kill / k8s hang until SIGKILL escalation — the exact K-REL-1 regression the pass-8 fix targeted, just for an uncovered prior-disposition value. The handler also never preserved/restored the original C handler, so it cannot be chained to. This is the precise teardown path the module docstring is concerned with. Realism: a C-installed SIGTERM handler before bootstrap is plausible (native runtimes/extensions such as gRPC/absl/TensorFlow or supervisor shims install handlers via sigaction, producing getsignal()==None); uncommon but real, with concrete operational impact (delayed/blocked shutdown, lost final span flush). The SIG_IGN fall-through is intentional and correct ("SIG_IGN stays ignored"), but the None case is a genuine unhandled gap. Proper fix: treat prior is None like SIG_DFL — reset to default and re-deliver so the process still dies on SIGTERM.

content.text: Verified TRUE_POSITIVE. The SIGTERM handler in `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py` (lines 159-163) does not handle `prior is None`, which `signal.getsignal()` returns for a C-installed handler — neither the `callable(prior)` chain branch nor the `prior == SIG_DFL` re-deliver branch fires, so SIGTERM is swallowed exactly as the K-REL-1 fix intended to prevent.
